preFix.title: Fitting a stair nosing strip to a timber stair
preFix.subtitle: Fit a metal or rubber nosing strip to protect and define a stair tread edge
preFix.excerpt: How to measure, cut, and fix an aluminium, brass, or rubber stair nosing strip to the front edge of a timber stair tread, using either screws countersunk into the strip or a contact adhesive, and how to align the strip so it sits flush and square across the full tread width.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Gradus stair nosing installation guide. Building Regulations Part K (2013): protection from falling, collision and impact.

content.text: protects the front edge of a timber tread. This edge takes heavy foot traffic and wears quickly, especially on the first two or three steps. Most domestic nosing strips are surface-mounted with screws. Building Regulations Part K requires the nosing to contrast visually with the tread. Check the strip colour against the stair finish before ordering.
Step 1, measure and cut the strip

Measure the width of the stair tread from string to string (or wall to wall). Cut the nosing strip to this length with a junior hacksaw or an angle grinder fitted with a metal-cutting disc. Remove any burrs from the cut end with the sanding block or a metal file. Dry-fit the strip in position and check it sits square across the full tread width.

Position the strip at the front edge of the tread so the leading face of the strip aligns with the nosing edge. Mark through the pre-drilled holes in the strip onto the tread with the pencil. Most strips are pre-drilled at 200 to 250 mm centres. If the strip is not pre-drilled, mark and drill holes at 200 mm centres with a metal drill bit of the correct diameter for the screw shank.
Step 3, drill the tread and countersink
Drill pilot holes in the tread at each marked position using a bit slightly smaller than the screw shank. Follow with a

to recess the screw head fully into the strip and below the tread surface. A proud screw head creates a trip hazard and will abrade footwear. Apply masking tape over the tread surface each side of the drill position to prevent tear-out on a painted or lacquered finish.

Apply a small bead of construction adhesive under the body of the strip for added security, then position the strip and drive the screws home. Do not overtighten: the strip is relatively thin and will distort if the screw is overtorqued. Check the strip lies flat along its full length. If one end lifts, the tread surface is slightly crowned; apply additional adhesive and clamp overnight.
Step 5, fit the anti-slip insert if supplied
Most aluminium and brass nosing strips include a rubber or carborundum

that clips or slides into a groove on the upper face of the strip. Press it in firmly along its full length; an insert that stops short will allow moisture under the exposed end and the insert will lift. Replace the insert when it becomes smooth rather than replacing the whole strip.

For a recessed nosing on a high-quality staircase, the tread face is routed with a slot the depth of the strip body and the strip is let in flush. This requires a router and a straight-edge guide and is an INTERMEDIATE woodworking task. On concrete or stone stairs, the strip is fixed with masonry anchors and epoxy adhesive rather than wood screws.

attrs.fix: Remove the strip, apply construction adhesive along the back face, refit, and clamp for 24 hours. Check all screws are fully driven.
attrs.cause: Tread surface not flat; adhesive insufficient; screw at end missed or stripped.
attrs.symptom: Strip rattles or springs up at one end after fitting

attrs.fix: Remove the screws, deepen the countersink with a countersink bit, and refit with flat-head countersunk screws.
attrs.cause: Countersink not deep enough; wrong screw head type (round head used instead of countersunk).
attrs.symptom: Screw heads proud of the strip surface

attrs.fix: Clean the groove, apply a small amount of contact adhesive inside the groove, press the insert back in, and tape until cured.
attrs.cause: Insert not fully seated; moisture or cleaning products getting under the exposed end.
attrs.symptom: Anti-slip insert lifts after a few months

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
